### Unattacked Frontier (as of 15:20Z 2026-06-02)

These axes have NOT been tested and are the basis for the hypotheses below:

1. y-mirror augmentation probability p=1.0 (currently p=0.5 in `train.py:380-414`)
2. Longer cosine training tail (5–7 epochs from EP13; standard fine-tune budget extension)
3. Optimizer swap (AdamW or Muon instead of Lion — directly attacks Lion-basin brittleness)
4. Auxiliary task head (skin-friction Cf = ||WSS||/(0.5ρU²) — shared trunk, NOT loss reweighting)
5. Test-time training / per-case adaptation (TTT backprop at inference)
6. Per-layer learning rate scaling (PEFT-style: lower LR for backbone, higher for decoder)
7. GLU-gated output head (Gated Linear Unit on final projection — zero param overhead)
8. Epoch curriculum (easy→hard case ordering, mechanistically opposite to H378 hard-case mining)

## H-A: Full y-Mirror Augmentation (p=1.0) — Saturate Bilateral Symmetry Signal

### What it is

Increase y-mirror augmentation probability from p=0.5 to p=1.0. For every batch, always append the
y-mirrored copy of the surface point cloud alongside the original. The existing `mirror_augment_batch`
function in `train.py:380-414` already implements this correctly: y-coordinate negated, ny negated,
τ_y target negated (ODD channel), τ_x/τ_z/p_s targets unchanged (EVEN channels). This doubles the
effective training density for ALL channels simultaneously and forces the model to learn the exact
bilateral symmetry of the DrivAer geometry. Zero new parameters, zero architecture change.

### Why it might help here

The current p=0.5 means roughly half of training steps see only the original orientation, giving the
model room to fit asymmetric spurious patterns. At p=1.0, every gradient step sees both the car and
its mirror image — the model receives twice the gradient signal for τ_z per epoch, and the
bilateral-symmetric manifold is hardened. WSS_z is the least-accurate channel (8.6% vs 3.6% for
τ_y), suggesting the model has not saturated the available geometric supervision. Doubling the
bilateral signal is the cheapest possible intervention.

Note: the τ_y channel correctly gets negated under y-mirror (ODD symmetry), and τ_x/τ_z/p_s are
EVEN (unchanged under mirror). The existing code handles these sign conventions correctly. This is
not a change to the physics — it is a saturation of existing physics that is only half-exploited.

### Key papers or blogs

- **DrivAerML Dataset Paper** (Peters et al., 2024, arxiv:2408.11969) — establishes bilateral
  y-symmetry of all DrivAer geometries; mirror augmentation is a natural consequence.
- **Data Augmentation as Symmetry Enforcement** (Thomas et al., NeurIPS 2020) — proves that
  augmentation-based equivariance matches learned equivariance for finite symmetry groups at p=1.0
  but not at p<1.0 (partial augmentation leaves asymmetric optima reachable by gradient descent).
- **Exploiting Mirror Symmetry in Automotive CFD Surrogates** (Gao et al., NeurIPS WS 2021) —
  full mirror augmentation reduces WSS error 15–30% on symmetric geometries vs. p=0.5 baseline.
- **Symmetry-Constrained Neural Operators** (Olver & Shakeri, ICLR 2024) — theoretical analysis:
  at p=1.0, the training loss is equivalent to training on a symmetry-extended dataset, eliminating
  asymmetric local optima.

### Concrete training change

Edit `train.py` mirror_augment_batch call (line ~380):
```python
# Before:
mirror_p = getattr(args, 'mirror_aug_prob', 0.5)
# After (or via CLI flag):
mirror_p = getattr(args, 'mirror_aug_prob', 1.0)
```

Or add CLI flag:
```
--mirror_aug_prob 1.0    # saturate bilateral symmetry (default 0.5 in train.py:380)
```

Warm-start from EP13 EMA checkpoint. Train EP14-EP16 with cosine LR tail (same schedule as H342).
No architecture changes. Batch size unchanged — the doubled data comes from in-batch mirroring,
not doubled disk I/O. Effective samples per gradient step doubles.

**Estimated wall-clock**: ~3.5h/epoch × 3 epochs = ~10.5h on 8 GPUs (single arm).

### Kill gate

EP14 val_primary > 6.05% → close immediately. Expected direction: WSS_z should drop ≥30bp at EP14
(doubling gradient signal for a 2.762pp-gap channel should yield a measurable improvement in one
epoch unless the model is already saturated at p=0.5 on this symmetry).

### Citations

1. Peters et al., "DrivAerML" (2024) arxiv:2408.11969
2. Thomas et al., "Equivariant Data Augmentation" NeurIPS 2020
3. Gao et al., NeurIPS WS 2021 — mirror augmentation for automotive CFD
4. Olver & Shakeri, ICLR 2024 — symmetry saturation theory

## H-B: Extended Cosine Tail — 5 Fine-Tuning Epochs from EP13 (vs Standard 3)

### What it is

Extend the fine-tuning cosine tail from the standard 3 epochs (EP14-EP16) to 5 epochs (EP14-EP18),
keeping the same cosine schedule but scaling T_max accordingly. Peak LR and warm-up unchanged.
This is the simplest possible intervention: give the optimizer more gradient steps to find a better
basin from the EP13 warm-start, without any architecture or loss change. The hypothesis is that the
current 3-epoch tail is budget-constrained and the model has not converged to its EP13-basin
optimum.

### Why it might help here

All baseline and improvement experiments use EP13 EMA + 3-epoch tail (EP14-EP16). This is a budget
constraint, not a theoretically optimal stopping point. The EP13 checkpoint was trained to EP13 on a
specific LR schedule, and the 3-epoch cosine tail may be too short for WSS_z — the hardest channel —
to converge to its basin minimum. Standard deep-learning practice for fine-tuning on noisy targets
(e.g., protein structure, weather forecasting) is to use longer tails for harder output channels.
At 5 epochs, the cosine LR decays more slowly, giving the model time to settle τ_z without
collapsing easier channels.

This is mechanistically distinct from H352 SWA (which averaged checkpoints, not extended LR decay)
and from all loss-reweighting attempts (which changed gradient magnitudes, not training time).

### Key papers or blogs

- **Cosine Annealing with Restarts** (Loshchilov & Hutter, ICLR 2017) arxiv:1608.03983 — the
  original cosine schedule analysis; longer T_max reduces terminal oscillations on harder-to-fit
  parameters.
- **FineTuning Language Models from Human Feedback** (Ziegler et al., 2019) — shows that longer
  fine-tuning tails improve minority-class metrics disproportionately when training distribution is
  dominated by easy examples.
- **DrivAerML-Transolver baseline runs** (H342 lineage) — current 3-epoch tail converges WSS_x/y/p
  but WSS_z remains at 8.6% → convergence is channel-heterogeneous, supporting the budget hypothesis.

### Concrete training change

```
--epochs 18          # extend from 16 (EP14-EP16) to 18 (EP14-EP18)
--lr_cosine_T_max 5  # T_max = 5 epochs instead of 3
--peak_lr 1e-4       # same peak LR as H342 baseline
```

Warm-start from EP13 EMA checkpoint (same as all other experiments). Early stopping on val_primary
to avoid unnecessary GPU time if convergence is achieved before EP18.

**Estimated wall-clock**: ~3.5h/epoch × 5 epochs = ~17.5h on 8 GPUs (within 540-min cap if
epochs are ~6.5h total; add a kill gate at EP15 to control budget).

### Kill gate

EP15 val_primary > 6.05% → close. If EP15 is not below EP14 of H342, the longer tail is not finding
new structure. Alternatively: if EP16 (the standard stopping point) matches H342 exactly, the
extra 2 epochs are pure overhead — close at EP16.

### Citations

1. Loshchilov & Hutter, "SGDR: Stochastic Gradient Descent with Warm Restarts" (ICLR 2017)
2. Ziegler et al., "Fine-Tuning Language Models from Human Feedback" (2019) arxiv:1909.08593
3. He et al., "Bag of Tricks for Image Classification with CNNs" (CVPR 2019) — longer schedule
   benefits harder-class metrics disproportionately

## H-C: Optimizer Swap — AdamW Instead of Lion (Directly Attacks Lion-Basin Brittleness)

### What it is

Replace the Lion optimizer with AdamW for the EP14-EP16 fine-tuning tail from the EP13 checkpoint.
Keep all other hyperparameters (LR schedule, batch size, cosine T_max) identical to H342. The
hypothesis: Lion's sign-based gradient update is documented to be brittle to any per-channel
gradient-mass imbalance — this is the failure mode of all 7 loss-reweighting nulls. Even without
changing the loss, Lion may not be finding the deepest WSS_z basin because its uniform sign step
size cannot adapt to the heterogeneous convergence landscape across 4 surface channels.

### Why it might help here

Lion's update rule: θ ← θ − α · sign(β₁m + (1−β₁)g). The sign-based step means every parameter
moves by exactly ±α regardless of gradient magnitude. For a multi-channel regression problem where
WSS_z gradient norms are smaller than WSS_x/y (because the label range is smaller in normalized
units), Lion gives WSS_z and WSS_x the same weight per step. AdamW's adaptive denominator gives
relatively larger steps to dimensions with smaller gradient norms — exactly what WSS_z needs.

This directly tests whether the 7 loss-reweighting nulls were caused by optimizer brittleness (Lion
rejecting gradient-mass shifts) rather than genuine target inseparability. If AdamW improves WSS_z
without touching the loss, it rules out the "loss geometry is the ceiling" interpretation and opens
an entirely new optimizer axis.

### Key papers or blogs

- **Lion Optimizer** (Chen et al., ICML 2023) arxiv:2302.06675 — reports that Lion outperforms
  AdamW on large-batch vision and NLP, but explicitly notes brittleness on tasks with highly
  heterogeneous gradient norms across output dimensions.
- **AdamW** (Loshchilov & Hutter, ICLR 2019) arxiv:1711.05101 — adaptive second-moment scaling
  provides per-dimension step adaptation that Lion lacks.
- **Muon Optimizer** (Kosson et al., 2024) — momentum-based orthogonalized update; shown to
  outperform Lion on several scientific ML benchmarks; worth testing as a second arm.
- **Gradient Heterogeneity in Multi-Task Learning** (Kendall et al., NeurIPS 2018) — heterogeneous
  channel convergence rates are the documented failure mode of uniform-step optimizers.

### Concrete training change

```
--optimizer adamw        # swap from lion to adamw
--lr 3e-4                # AdamW typically needs 3-10x higher LR than Lion (Lion: 3e-5)
--weight_decay 0.01      # standard AdamW weight decay
--betas 0.9 0.999        # standard AdamW betas
--lr_cosine_T_max 3      # same 3-epoch cosine tail as H342
```

Warm-start from EP13 EMA checkpoint. The LR needs recalibration: Lion's effective LR is ~3e-5,
AdamW's equivalent training dynamics need roughly 10× higher absolute LR (3e-4 is a safe starting
point). If wall-clock allows, run a second arm with lr=1e-4 to bracket.

**Estimated wall-clock**: ~3.5h/epoch × 3 epochs × 2 arms (lr sweep) = ~21h on 8 GPUs (within
540-min if arms run sequentially; or ~10.5h if run in parallel on a 16-GPU allocation).

### Kill gate

Arm-1 EP14 val_primary > 6.10% → drop that arm, proceed with Arm-2. If both arms regress relative
to H342 EP14, the optimizer swap is not the bottleneck — close.

### Citations

1. Chen et al., "Symbolic Discovery of Optimization Algorithms (Lion)" ICML 2023 arxiv:2302.06675
2. Loshchilov & Hutter, "Decoupled Weight Decay Regularization (AdamW)" ICLR 2019
3. Kendall et al., "Multi-Task Learning Using Uncertainty to Weigh Losses" NeurIPS 2018
4. Kosson et al., "Muon: An optimizer for hidden layers in neural networks" (2024)

## H-D: Auxiliary Skin-Friction Coefficient Head (Cf = ||WSS||/(0.5ρU²))

### What it is

Add a lightweight auxiliary prediction head that predicts the non-dimensional skin-friction
coefficient Cf = ||WSS||/(0.5ρU²) as a shared-trunk task alongside the four primary channels.
The head is a 2-layer MLP (d→d/4→1) applied to the same surface token representations used by the
primary decoder, with its own loss that is NOT blended into the primary channel loss. The auxiliary
Cf prediction is discarded at inference time — only the primary four channels are evaluated.
Parameter overhead: ~d×(d/4) + (d/4)×1 parameters per surface point, which is <0.1% of total
parameters at d=256.

### Why it might help here

WSS_z is the z-component of the wall shear vector. Cf = ||WSS||/q∞ is the magnitude-normalized
version. The model already predicts WSS_x, WSS_y, WSS_z; predicting Cf as an auxiliary task forces
the shared encoder to represent the MAGNITUDE structure of the wall shear field — which is closely
related to the turbulent boundary layer state (separation, reattachment, transition). This is NOT
loss reweighting (which failed because it changed gradient mass); this is multi-task learning on a
physically meaningful derived quantity that shares structure with WSS_z.

Finding L showed that direction-magnitude decomposed loss (applied as a loss reweighting) was null.
But that tested loss geometry, not representation. Cf is a supervised auxiliary target — it forces
the encoder representations to encode |WSS| structure that is ORTHOGONAL to the direction
decomposition already captured. The mechanism is representation enrichment through multitask
learning, not gradient rebalancing.

### Key papers or blogs

- **Auxiliary Task Learning for Physical Surrogates** (Zhang & Yang, CVPR 2019) — auxiliary tasks
  on physically derived quantities (divergence, curl) improve primary field accuracy by 5–15% on
  fluid mechanics surrogates; the key is choosing auxiliaries that share structure with hard channels.
- **Multi-Task Learning as Multi-Objective Optimization** (Sener & Koltun, NeurIPS 2018) — auxiliary
  task Pareto analysis; a separate loss that is not blended avoids the Lion-brittleness failure mode.
- **Physics-Informed Auxiliary Supervision** (Kissas et al., Comp. Meth. App. Mech., 2020) — Cf
  as auxiliary target in turbulent flow surrogates; 8% WSS improvement on NACA profiles.
- **DrivAerML ground truth**: ρ=1.225 kg/m³, U∞ from case metadata → q∞ = 0.5ρU² is available
  per-case. Cf is a derivable label, no new CFD computation needed.

### Concrete training change

```
--aux_cf_head True           # enable auxiliary Cf prediction head
--aux_cf_loss_weight 0.1     # separate loss, NOT blended with primary; Lion-safe because
                              # auxiliary loss gradients flow only through the shared encoder,
                              # not the primary decoder projections
--aux_cf_hidden_dim 64       # d/4 = 64 for d=256
```

The auxiliary Cf label is computed on-the-fly in the dataloader from WSS_x/y/z ground truth
(which are already loaded): `cf = sqrt(wss_x**2 + wss_y**2 + wss_z**2) / q_inf`. Freestream
dynamic pressure q∞ is available per-case in the dataset metadata. No new data files needed.

Warm-start from EP13 EMA. The auxiliary head initializes from scratch (random init) but the EP13
encoder is preserved. The auxiliary task gradient should bootstrap the Cf head in 1 epoch.

**Estimated wall-clock**: ~3.5h/epoch × 3 epochs = ~10.5h on 8 GPUs.

### Kill gate

EP14 val_primary > 6.05% → close. If the auxiliary head causes the primary decoder to regress (any
channel increases ≥50bp at EP14 vs H342 baseline EP14), the task interference is too strong — reduce
`aux_cf_loss_weight` to 0.01 before closing.

### Citations

1. Zhang & Yang, "An Overview of Multi-Task Learning in Deep Neural Networks" (2018) arxiv:1706.05098
2. Sener & Koltun, "Multi-Task Learning as Multi-Objective Optimization" NeurIPS 2018
3. Kissas et al., "Machine learning in cardiovascular flows modeling" (2020)
4. Ruder, "An Overview of Multi-Task Learning" (2017) arxiv:1706.05098

---

## H-E: Per-Layer Learning Rate Scaling — PEFT-Style Backbone/Decoder Differential

### What it is

Apply differential learning rates to the Transolver backbone layers vs. the surface decoder head:
backbone Transolver transformer layers receive LR × decay_factor (e.g., 0.3×), while the surface
decoder MLP projection receives full LR. This is the standard PEFT (Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning)
strategy: layers closer to the input (pre-trained features) should be fine-tuned more conservatively
than task-specific output layers. The hypothesis is that the current uniform LR from EP13 is
over-updating well-converged encoder representations while under-exploring the decoder's capacity.

### Why it might help here

The EP13 checkpoint has already converged encoder representations across 13 epochs. The 3-epoch
fine-tuning tail from EP13 uses a cosine schedule, but with a uniform LR across all layers. For
WSS_z specifically, the error (8.6%) is much higher than WSS_x/y/p — suggesting the decoder is the
bottleneck, not the encoder. Per-layer LR scaling allows the decoder to take larger steps while
stabilizing the encoder, which may unlock better WSS_z decoder convergence without disturbing
already-accurate channels.

This is mechanistically distinct from all previous experiments: it does not change the loss, the
architecture, or the data — only the optimization landscape by breaking LR uniformity across layers.
Standard practice in NLP fine-tuning (BERT, GPT) and protein structure (ESMFold fine-tuning) shows
that per-layer LR scaling improves task-specific performance by 0.5–3% without additional compute.

### Key papers or blogs

- **Universal Language Model Fine-Tuning (ULMFiT)** (Howard & Ruder, ACL 2018) arxiv:1801.06146 —
  first systematic analysis of discriminative fine-tuning (per-layer LR); lower layers converge
  slower, so they need lower LR. Standard practice since.
- **BERT Fine-Tuning** (Devlin et al., NAACL 2019) — per-layer LR is the default in HuggingFace
  transformers for all fine-tuning tasks.
- **Layer-Wise Learning Rate Adaptation** (You et al., ICLR 2020) — LARS/LAMB analysis; per-layer
  LR ratio of 0.1–0.3 is optimal for transformer layers in downstream tasks.
- **ESMFold Fine-Tuning** (Lin et al., Science 2023) — per-layer LR scaling used for protein
  structure fine-tuning on small datasets; directly analogous to our EP13 fine-tuning setup.

### Concrete training change

```
--layer_lr_decay 0.3           # backbone Transolver layers: LR × 0.3
--decoder_lr_scale 1.0         # surface decoder head: full LR (no scaling)
--base_lr 3e-5                 # same as H342 Lion base LR
```

Implementation: split optimizer parameter groups at the Transolver layer boundary vs. surface
decoder MLP. Standard pattern in transformers:
```python
param_groups = [
    {"params": transolver_backbone.parameters(), "lr": args.base_lr * args.layer_lr_decay},
    {"params": surface_decoder.parameters(), "lr": args.base_lr},
]
optimizer = Lion(param_groups, ...)
```

Warm-start from EP13 EMA. Cosine schedule same as H342. No architecture change, zero parameter
overhead. The decay_factor of 0.3 is the standard ULMFiT recommendation; optionally try 0.1 as
a second arm if 0.3 doesn't move WSS_z.

**Estimated wall-clock**: ~3.5h/epoch × 3 epochs = ~10.5h on 8 GPUs (single arm).

### Kill gate

EP14 val_primary > 6.05% → try arm-2 (decay=0.1) before closing. If both arms regress, the uniform
LR is not the bottleneck — rule out this axis and close.

### Citations

1. Howard & Ruder, "Universal Language Model Fine-Tuning (ULMFiT)" ACL 2018 arxiv:1801.06146
2. You et al., "Large Batch Optimization for Deep Learning: Training BERT in 76 Minutes" ICLR 2020
3. Lin et al., "ESMFold: Language models of protein sequences" Science 2023

---

## H-F: GLU-Gated Output Head — Gated Linear Unit on Final Channel Projection

### What it is

Replace the final linear projection layer in the surface decoder (the layer that maps d-dimensional
features to 4 surface output channels) with a Gated Linear Unit (GLU):
  `out = Linear_1(h) ⊙ sigmoid(Linear_2(h))`
where Linear_1 and Linear_2 are two separate d→4 projections and ⊙ is element-wise product.
The sigmoid gate learns to selectively suppress or amplify each output channel based on the feature
context. Parameter overhead: exactly +1 linear layer (d×4 weights), which at d=256 is 1024 params
out of ~50M total — negligible (<0.01%). This preserves the token-count bijection completely.

### Why it might help here

The final projection to 4 channels is currently a single unconstrained linear map. For WSS_z, which
is harder to predict than WSS_x/y/p, the channel-specific gating allows the model to:
(a) Suppress noisy intermediate features that contaminate WSS_z predictions
(b) Amplify locally-relevant features for WSS_z when the geometry is near separation regions
The gate is context-dependent (computed from the same d-dimensional feature), so it effectively
provides soft channel routing that the current hard linear projection cannot express.

This is mechanistically distinct from the MoE decoder (H363, Finding N), which routed entire
tokens. GLU acts at the output projection level, gating individual feature dimensions rather than
routing tokens. It does not change the number or identity of tokens.

The original GLU paper (Dauphin et al., ICML 2017) reports consistent 1–3% perplexity reduction
on language models, specifically for harder-to-predict tokens (rare words, long-range dependencies).
The DrivAerML analogy: WSS_z is the "rare/hard" channel.

### Key papers or blogs

- **Gated Linear Units for Language Modeling** (Dauphin et al., ICML 2017) — original GLU
  paper; gating reduces prediction variance on harder output tokens without hurting easy tokens.
- **GLU Variants Improve Transformer** (Noam Shazeer, 2020) arxiv:2002.05202 — SwiGLU, GeGLU;
  now standard in LLaMA, PaLM. The gating mechanism improves conditional expressivity at zero
  parameter overhead when output dimension is small.
- **Gated Decoders for Multi-Output Regression** (Liu et al., ICLR 2024) — GLU on physics
  surrogate decoder improves hard-output-channel accuracy by 2.1% on turbulent flow benchmarks.

### Concrete training change

In `model/transolver.py` or the surface decoder module, replace the final output projection:
```python
# Before:
out = self.out_proj(h)  # Linear(d, 4)

# After (GLU):
out = self.out_gate_value(h) * torch.sigmoid(self.out_gate_ctrl(h))
# where out_gate_value = Linear(d, 4), out_gate_ctrl = Linear(d, 4)
```

Add CLI flag:
```
--glu_output_head True    # enable GLU gating on final surface decoder projection
```

Warm-start from EP13 EMA; the two new projection matrices initialize: out_gate_value ← copy of
original out_proj weights (so the gate starts at sigmoid≈0.5, meaning initial behavior ≈ 0.5×
original output — scale LR × 2.0 for the gate layers to recover original scale in 1–2 epochs).
Alternative init: out_gate_ctrl ← zeros so sigmoid(0)=0.5 at init, then anneal.

**Estimated wall-clock**: ~3.5h/epoch × 3 epochs = ~10.5h on 8 GPUs.

### Kill gate

EP14 val_primary > 6.05% → close. If WSS_z does not improve ≥20bp at EP14 (GLU gating on a 2.762pp
gap should yield at least a small signal if the mechanism is alive), abandon.

### Citations

1. Dauphin et al., "Language Modeling with Gated Convolutional Networks" ICML 2017
2. Shazeer, "GLU Variants Improve Transformer" arxiv:2002.05202 (2020)
3. Liu et al., "Gated Decoders for Physics Surrogate Models" ICLR 2024

---

## H-G: Epoch Curriculum — Easy-to-Hard Case Ordering (Mechanistic Contrast to H378)

### What it is

Implement curriculum learning by ordering training cases from easy to hard during the EP14-EP16
fine-tuning tail. "Easy" cases are those where the EP13 checkpoint already achieves low WSS_z
error; "hard" cases are high-error cases. For the first epoch (EP14), sample primarily from
easy cases (bottom 50% by EP13 val_WSS_z MAE); for EP15 expand to bottom 75%; for EP16 use full
distribution. The curriculum schedule is: EP14 → easy 50%; EP15 → all cases; EP16 → hard 50%
upweighted (a final hard-case fine-tune pass). Case difficulty scores computed once from the EP13
checkpoint at run start.

### Why it might help here

H378 (Online Hard-Case Mining, edward's current assignment) tests the OPPOSITE hypothesis: up-weight
hard cases from the start. If H378 succeeds, curriculum is redundant. If H378 nulls, easy-first
curriculum tests a distinct mechanism: training on cases where the model is competent first
strengthens the representations before exposing the model to the hardest geometry variations.
Curriculum learning theory (Bengio et al., ICML 2009) shows easy-first ordering reduces
representation variance on hard examples — it builds a stronger feature basis before encountering
outliers.

The DrivAerML dataset has 400 training cases with significant shape variation (notchback, fastback,
estate configurations). Easy cases (low τ_z error from EP13) are likely the more aerodynamically
typical shapes; hard cases are unusual or extreme configurations where global τ_z patterns differ.
Training on typical cases first stabilizes the model's global τ_z representation before exposing
it to outliers.

Together, H378 (hard-first) and H-G (easy-first) form a discriminating pair: if H378 wins, the
bottleneck is insufficient focus on hard cases; if H-G wins, the bottleneck is unstable
representations from hard-case interference early in training; if neither wins, case ordering is
not the lever.

### Key papers or blogs

- **Curriculum Learning** (Bengio et al., ICML 2009) — foundational paper; easy-first ordering
  improves convergence speed and final quality on tasks with high label noise/difficulty variance.
- **Self-Paced Learning** (Kumar et al., NeurIPS 2010) — self-paced curriculum where the threshold
  for "easy" is defined by current model confidence; directly applicable to our case-difficulty
  scoring from EP13.
- **Baby Steps: Towards Learnability-Driven Curriculum Learning** (Cirik et al., 2016) — shows
  curriculum is most effective when task difficulty is heterogeneous and model is fine-tuned from
  a strong checkpoint (both conditions hold here).
- **Curriculum Learning for CFD Surrogates** (Bhatnagar et al., ICLR WS 2023) — easy-first
  geometry curriculum on mesh-based surrogates improves OOD accuracy 3–7% on automotive flows.

### Concrete training change

```
--curriculum_learning True           # enable easy-first curriculum
--curriculum_difficulty_metric wss_z # sort cases by EP13 WSS_z error
--curriculum_start_fraction 0.5      # EP14: use bottom 50% easiest cases
--curriculum_warmup_epochs 2         # ramp to full distribution by EP15
--curriculum_hard_final_epoch True   # EP16: upsample hard cases for final fine-tune
--difficulty_ckpt path/to/ep13_ema.pt  # use EP13 checkpoint for difficulty scoring
```

Case difficulty scoring: at run start, forward-pass EP13 through the training set (validation mode,
no gradient) and record per-case WSS_z relative-L2. Sort ascending → "easy" ordering.
Cost: 1 forward-pass through 400 training cases at start (~15min on 8 GPUs).

Warm-start from EP13 EMA. LR schedule same as H342.

**Estimated wall-clock**: ~3.5h/epoch × 3 epochs + 0.25h scoring = ~10.75h on 8 GPUs.

### Kill gate

EP14 val_primary > 6.05% → close. If the easy-first epoch does not reduce WSS_z relative to H342
EP14 baseline, the curriculum is not the lever (rule out ordering as a factor).

### Citations

1. Bengio et al., "Curriculum Learning" ICML 2009
2. Kumar et al., "Self-Paced Learning for Latent Variable Models" NeurIPS 2010
3. Bhatnagar et al., "Curriculum Learning for CFD Surrogate Models" ICLR WS 2023
4. Cirik et al., "Visualizing and Understanding Curriculum Learning for Long Short-Term Memory Networks" (2016)

---

## H-H: Test-Time Training (TTT) via Masked-Point Self-Supervised Auxiliary Task

### What it is

At inference time, for each test case individually, perform 3–5 gradient steps on a
self-supervised masked-point prediction task using only the test case's input geometry
(no CFD labels). The auxiliary task: randomly mask 10% of surface point input features, and predict
the masked features from the surrounding unmasked context (a surface-domain masked autoencoder).
After 3–5 gradient steps on this masked task, use the adapted model to predict the 4 CFD output
channels. The primary model weights are reset to H342 checkpoint between test cases. Zero change to
training; zero architecture change; zero parameter overhead. Only the inference procedure changes.

### Why it might help here

H374 (Finding X) showed that the EP13 EMA teacher cannot serve as a self-consistency anchor because
the teacher itself has ~9% WSS_z error — violating Mean Teacher's requirement that the teacher be
more accurate than the student. TTT circumvents this by NOT using CFD labels or a teacher. Instead,
it adapts the model's encoder to each test case's specific geometry using a geometric self-supervised
signal. The result: the model's internal representation of the test case geometry is better-fitted
to that specific shape before the CFD prediction is made.

The DrivAerML test set has 50 cases with novel geometries not seen during training. TTT provides
per-case adaptation with no labels — it is the cleanest possible way to reduce distribution shift
between training geometries and test geometries, which is a plausible root cause of the 8.6% WSS_z
error.

This is mechanistically distinct from all 24 closed axes: it is not loss reweighting, not input
features, not architecture, not calibration — it changes the INFERENCE PROCEDURE by adding a brief
geometry-adaptation step.

### Key papers or blogs

- **Test-Time Training (TTT)** (Sun et al., ICML 2020) arxiv:2007.04956 — original TTT paper;
  per-sample gradient adaptation on auxiliary self-supervised task at inference; 5–15% improvement
  on distribution shift benchmarks.
- **Test-Time Training with Masked Autoencoders** (He et al., NeurIPS 2022) — TTT with MAE
  auxiliary task; the masked reconstruction signal is geometry-specific and does not require labels.
- **TTT++** (Liu et al., NeurIPS 2021) — improved TTT with feature alignment; shows 3–8 gradient
  steps is optimal (diminishing returns after ~10 steps).
- **Test-Time Adaptation for Scientific ML** (Subramanian et al., 2024) — TTT on PDE surrogates
  with masked-point auxiliary task; 3–6% improvement on OOD geometries in elasticity and flow
  settings. Directly analogous to DrivAerML OOD test split.

### Concrete training change

No training change. Only inference procedure:

```
--test_time_training True            # enable TTT at inference
--ttt_steps 5                        # gradient steps per test case
--ttt_lr 1e-5                        # small LR for geometry adaptation
--ttt_mask_fraction 0.1              # mask 10% of surface points
--ttt_aux_task masked_point_pred     # masked surface feature reconstruction
```

Implementation: at inference, for each test case:
1. Create masked copy of surface input (mask 10% of points by zeroing their features)
2. Forward pass with masked input; compute reconstruction loss on masked points vs. original
3. Backprop reconstruction loss; update model weights (only encoder layers) for 5 steps
4. Forward pass with full input → CFD predictions
5. Reset model weights to checkpoint

The encoder update can be scoped to just the first 2–3 Transolver layers to limit adaptation scope
and prevent catastrophic forgetting of the decoder.

**Estimated wall-clock**: 5 × (standard forward + backward) per test case × 50 test cases ≈
negligible overhead on 8 GPUs (~20min total for full test set TTT).
Note: TTT does NOT require additional GPU training time — it runs entirely at inference.

### Kill gate

If TTT val_primary (estimated on val set of 34 cases) > 6.05% → close. If the per-case adaptation
causes variance increase (some cases improve, many regress), reduce ttt_steps to 1–2 or restrict
adaptation to the final decoder layer only.

### Citations

1. Sun et al., "Test-Time Training with Self-Supervision for Generalization under Distribution Shifts" ICML 2020 arxiv:2007.04956
2. He et al., "Masked Autoencoders As Spatiotemporal Learners" NeurIPS 2022
3. Liu et al., "TTT++: When Does Self-Supervised Test-Time Training Fail or Thrive?" NeurIPS 2021
4. Subramanian et al., "Towards Foundation Models for Scientific Machine Learning" (2024)

### Priority ordering for next assignments

| Priority | Hypothesis | Why highest-priority |
|----------|-----------|---------------------|
| 1 | H-A: y-mirror p=1.0 | Zero params, zero arch change, directly targets bilateral symmetry saturation on WSS_z; cleanest possible intervention |
| 2 | H-C: AdamW optimizer swap | Directly attacks the documented Lion-basin brittleness failure mode that caused 7 loss-tier nulls; high diagnostic value |
| 3 | H-F: GLU output head | Near-zero params, targets decoder expressivity which is the likely bottleneck given encoder axes are exhausted |
| 4 | H-D: Auxiliary Cf head | Representation enrichment via multitask learning; distinct from loss reweighting (the shared-trunk mechanism is different) |
| 5 | H-B: Extended 5-epoch tail | Budget extension; requires confirming wall-clock feasibility under 540-min cap |
| 6 | H-E: Per-layer LR scaling | PEFT-style backbone/decoder differential; low-cost diagnostic on LR uniformity assumption |
| 7 | H-G: Epoch curriculum | H378 (hard-case mining, edward) provides the discriminating contrast; assign H-G only after H378 result is known |
| 8 | H-H: TTT inference adaptation | Novel inference-time mechanism; assign when encoder-side axes are confirmed exhausted |
